title: Opinion: Can churches renew the ‘Pursuit of Happiness’? Building a movement to fix the American family | Institute for Family Studies
gist: This IFS 'In The News' page is a truncated excerpt (cut off mid-argument at 'Continues...') of a Deseret News op-ed by Brad Wilcox and James Lynch: 250 years after Jefferson enshrined 'the pursuit of Happiness,' the piece opens by noting the U.S. is losing that pursuit, citing near-record depression rates, a low World Happiness Ranking versus peer-wealth nations, and historically low optimism; the op-ed's full argument about churches renewing family life is not captured in this excerpt.

insights.claim: The U.S. depression rate is near record highs, per a Deseret News op-ed by Brad Wilcox and James Lynch.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: A headline well-being metric relevant to any thesis linking family/social breakdown to mental-health spending or social-cohesion policy.
insights.quote: The U.S. depression rate is near record highs.

insights.claim: The World Happiness Rankings place the United States much lower than countries of comparable wealth.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Highlights a wealth-happiness decoupling in the U.S. relative to peer economies, a data point for social-cohesion and well-being-linked policy discourse.
insights.quote: The World Happiness Rankings put America much lower than countries of comparable wealth.

insights.claim: American optimism is at historic lows, two and a half centuries after the Declaration of Independence enshrined 'the pursuit of Happiness.'
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Frames the op-ed's premise (churches/community as a possible fix) against measurably declining national sentiment, relevant to social-cohesion and philanthropy-linked discourse.
